cards.front: What are the main steps in developing a C++ program?
cards.back:
1. Define the problem to solve
  2. Design the solution
  3. Write the code
  4. Compile the code
  5. Link object files
  6. Test and debug

cards.text: Before writing code, you should {{c1::define the problem}} you're trying to solve and {{c2::design a solution}} approach.

cards.front: Why is it important to design your solution before coding?
cards.back: Good design helps you understand the problem, plan the structure, and avoid wasting time fixing poorly thought-out code. It's much easier to fix design issues before coding than after.

cards.text: Most development time is spent {{c1::testing and debugging}} rather than {{c2::writing new code}}.

cards.front: What is an IDE (Integrated Development Environment)?
cards.back: A software application that combines a code editor, compiler, linker, and debugger into one integrated package for easier development.

cards.concept: Advantages of using an IDE vs separate tools
cards.explanation:
IDEs integrate all development tools in one place:
  • Automatic project management
  • Built-in compiler and build system
  • Integrated debugger with breakpoints
  • Code completion and syntax highlighting
  • Error detection as you type
You don't need to manually run compiler commands or configure build systems.

cards.text: Popular C++ IDEs include {{c1::Visual Studio}} for Windows, {{c2::Xcode}} for macOS, and {{c3::Code::Blocks}} for cross-platform development.

cards.front: What is the difference between an IDE and a code editor like VS Code?
cards.back: An IDE has compiler, debugger, and build tools built-in. A code editor is lighter-weight and requires installing and configuring external tools (compiler, debugger) separately.

cards.front: Why should beginners use a full IDE rather than just a text editor?
cards.back: IDEs provide better error messages, integrated debugging, automatic project setup, and handle build configuration. This lets beginners focus on learning C++ rather than toolchain setup.

cards.text: The typical development cycle is: {{c1::write code}} → {{c2::compile}} → {{c3::test}} → {{c4::debug}} → repeat.

cards.front: What is the difference between testing and debugging?
cards.back: Testing is running the program to check if it works correctly. Debugging is finding and fixing the bugs discovered during testing.

cards.explanation:
Don't try to write an entire program at once. Instead:
  • Write small pieces of functionality
  • Compile and test frequently
  • Fix errors immediately before adding more code
  • This makes bugs easier to find and fix
examples: Write and test one function at a time
Compile after adding each new feature
Don't wait until the end to test everything
